title: Anaemia
subtitle: Haemoglobin below normal
patientDescription: Enter your haemoglobin and related results to find out what the NHS guideline recommends.
clinicianDescription: NCL primary care pathway for anaemia. Hb <110 g/L in adults. Branches on MCV, reticulocyte count, B12/folate, and iron studies.

source.document: Abnormal Full Blood Count (FBC) in Adults — Primary Care Clinical Pathway

background.definition: Anaemia defined as Hb <110 g/L in men and non-pregnant women over 15 years of age (NCL ICB). NB: NWL threshold is <130 g/L men, <114 g/L women.
primaryCareAssessment: Symptoms of anaemia such as fatigue, SOBOE, headache, dizziness, cognitive dysfunction, restless leg syndrome
Any symptoms of bleeding including menstrual history
Diet, medication (NSAIDs/steroids), alcohol, family history, recent transfusion, travel history, ethnic origin
Examine for pallor, jaundice; in Fe deficiency — koilonychia, angular cheilosis, glossitis and diffuse and moderate alopecia
Monitor FBC for trend/evidence of progression over time (depends on severity, but if >100, usually over 3 months)
Repeat FBC, blood film, reticulocytes, U+Es, creatinine, LFT, ferritin*, B12*, folate*, CRP — *assess in all cases of anaemia irrespective of MCV

nodes.description: Emergency — check if Hb critically low or very symptomatic

nodes.verbatim: If Hb <50g/l or very symptomatic — consider admission. Urgently/2ww refer to haematology: leukoerythroblastic anaemia on blood film, unexplained progressive symptomatic anaemia, associated splenomegaly, lymphadenopathy and other cytopenias, suspected haematological malignancy or other blood disorders (2WW). Urgently/2ww refer to GI/Colorectal: acute GI bleeding, >60yrs with IDA, <50yrs with IDA and rectal bleeding.
nodes.patientSummary: Your haemoglobin is very low. You may need urgent hospital assessment today.

referrals.verbatim: Urgently/2ww refer to haematology if: leukoerythroblastic anaemia on blood film, unexplained progressive symptomatic anaemia, associated splenomegaly, lymphadenopathy and other cytopenias, suspected haematological malignancy (2WW)

nodes.description: Branch on MCV to determine anaemia type
Check iron studies and CRP to classify microcytic anaemia

branches.label: High ferritin >150 mcg/L — Unlikely iron deficiency

nodes.verbatim: Iron deficiency anaemia (IDA) — See Iron Deficiency Pathway. Ensure Coeliac screen done if unexplained iron deficiency. Commence appropriate treatment and monitor response.
nodes.patientSummary: Your results suggest iron deficiency anaemia. Your GP should start iron treatment and investigate the cause.

referrals.verbatim: All men and postmenopausal women with iron deficiency anaemia — refer to gastroenterology unless overt non-gastrointestinal bleeding. See NCL Iron Deficiency Pathway.

investigations.label: Iron studies and CRP
investigations.verbatim: Check iron studies and CRP. Note: Ferritin can be raised in the presence of acute or chronic inflammation, infection, malignant disease or liver disease.

investigations.label: Coeliac screen
investigations.verbatim: Ensure Coeliac screen done if unexplained iron deficiency

investigations.label: Urine dipstick
investigations.verbatim: Urine dipstick — 1% will have renal malignancy

notes: Other RBC changes that may be seen: reduced mean cell Hb (hypochromia), anisocytosis, poikilocytosis
Ferritin can be falsely elevated by inflammation — formal iron studies may be more useful

nodes.verbatim: Indeterminate ferritin 30–150 mcg/L with no known inflammatory states. If CRP normal, iron >7 µmol/L, iron saturation >20%, TIBC >45 µmol/L → iron replete. If CRP >30mg/L, iron <7 µmol/L, iron saturation <20%, TIBC <45 µmol/L → functional iron deficiency — refer to Iron Deficiency Pathway.
nodes.patientSummary: Your ferritin is in a borderline range. Your GP needs further iron tests (including CRP) to interpret this properly.

investigations.label: Formal iron studies (iron, TIBC, transferrin saturation)
CRP
investigations.verbatim: Ferritin can be raised in presence of acute or chronic inflammation — CRP needed to interpret

nodes.verbatim: High ferritin >150 mcg/L — interpret with other iron study indices e.g. transferrin saturation, but unlikely iron deficiency. Consider: non-iron deficient microcytic anaemia or functional iron deficiency. Non-haematological causes: acute/chronic inflammation, chronic infection, malignancy, liver disease, renal failure, anaemia of chronic disease. Haematological causes: haemoglobinopathy (e.g. thalassaemia trait) — screen if no previous Hb electrophoresis and ongoing microcytic parameters with replete Fe stores. Sideroblastic anaemia.
nodes.patientSummary: Your ferritin is high, making iron deficiency unlikely as the sole cause of your anaemia. Your GP needs to investigate other causes.

referrals.verbatim: Refer to appropriate specialist in secondary care (2ww, urgent, routine where clinically indicated) or if remains unexplained

investigations.label: Haemoglobin electrophoresis (if no previous result and ongoing microcytic parameters)
investigations.verbatim: Screen if no previous Hb electrophoresis and ongoing microcytic parameters with replete Fe stores

nodes.description: Normocytic anaemia — check if isolated or with other cytopenias

branches.label: Other cytopenia present (low platelets) — refer urgently

nodes.verbatim: Refer urgently if pancytopenia — normocytic anaemia with other cytopenias suggests bone marrow pathology.
nodes.patientSummary: Your blood count shows more than one type of cell is low. This needs urgent specialist assessment.

investigations.label: Myeloma screen (protein electrophoresis + free light chains)

nodes.description: Isolated normocytic anaemia — check reticulocyte count

nodes.verbatim: Isolated normocytic anaemia, reticulocyte count <80×10⁹/L (<2%). Consider: haematological disorder e.g. MDS, myeloma. Investigate and manage where appropriate. Refer to secondary care (2ww, urgent, routine where clinically indicated) or if remains unexplained.
nodes.patientSummary: Your anaemia appears to be due to the bone marrow not making enough red blood cells. Your GP should investigate the cause.

referrals.verbatim: Refer to appropriate specialist in secondary care (2ww, urgent, routine where clinically indicated) or if remains unexplained. Consider A&G/Consultant Connect.

investigations.label: Myeloma screen (protein electrophoresis + free light chains)
B12 and folate
investigations.verbatim: Assess in all cases of anaemia irrespective of MCV

nodes.verbatim: Normocytic anaemia with reticulocyte count >80×10⁹/L (>2%) — consider haemorrhage or haemolysis (bilirubin elevated). Investigate and manage where appropriate.
nodes.patientSummary: Your results suggest your bone marrow is working hard to compensate. This can happen with bleeding or red blood cell destruction. Urgent assessment is needed.

referrals.verbatim: Consider haemolysis — urgent review if bilirubin elevated

nodes.description: Check B12 and folate in macrocytic anaemia

nodes.verbatim: B12 <170ng/l and symptomatic — commence B12 replacement. B12 <170ng/l and asymptomatic — recheck 2 months. If still low check Intrinsic factor antibody and treat. B12 >170ng/l but strong index clinical suspicion — check Intrinsic factor antibody. If positive — lifelong B12 replacement.
nodes.patientSummary: Your B12 is low. Your GP should start B12 treatment. B12 deficiency can cause anaemia and nerve problems.

investigations.label: Intrinsic factor antibody (if strong clinical suspicion or persistent low B12)
investigations.verbatim: Check Intrinsic factor antibody — if positive lifelong B12 replacement

investigations.label: Folate
investigations.verbatim: Check B12 level in all people before starting folic acid

notes: Clinical features of B12 deficiency are variable.
Do not perform B12 testing in pregnancy as results are unreliable.
Intrinsic factor antibodies are present in up to 35% of pernicious anaemia cases — a negative result is unhelpful.

nodes.verbatim: Serum folate <3µg/l or 3–4.5µg/l and symptomatic. Aetiology: deficient diet, excess alcohol, malabsorption, medication, metabolic, excess urinary excretion, CHF, chronic dialysis, liver disease. Ensure B12 normal/coeliac screen. Folate replacement — monitor response.
nodes.patientSummary: Your folate is low. Your GP should start folic acid treatment after checking your B12 level.

investigations.label: B12 (must be checked before starting folate)
investigations.verbatim: Check vitamin B12 level in all people before starting folic acid — treatment can improve wellbeing, mask underlying B12 deficiency and allow neurological disease to develop

nodes.verbatim: Normal B12/folate and asymptomatic of B12/folate deficiency. Consider: alcohol, hypothyroidism, drugs, liver disease, pregnancy, haematological disorder e.g. MDS, myeloma. Investigate and manage where appropriate. Refer to secondary care (2ww, urgent, routine where clinically indicated) or if remains unexplained. Consider A&G/Consultant Connect.
nodes.patientSummary: Your B12 and folate are normal, so there must be another cause for your large red blood cells. Your GP needs to investigate further — possible causes include alcohol, thyroid problems or, rarely, blood disorders.

referrals.verbatim: Refer to appropriate specialist in secondary care. Consider A&G/Consultant Connect.

investigations.label: LFTs and TFTs
investigations.verbatim: Consider alcohol, hypothyroidism, drugs, liver disease

investigations.label: Myeloma screen (protein electrophoresis + free light chains)

nodes.verbatim: Reticulocytosis without evidence of bleeding — consider advice and guidance/referral.
nodes.patientSummary: Your body is producing many immature red blood cells, which can happen when red blood cells are being destroyed. Urgent assessment is needed.

referrals.verbatim: Reticulocytosis without evidence of bleeding — consider advice and guidance/referral to haematology
